mythological_punishment: Seasonal compromise involving Persephone returning for part of the year
modern_uk_equivalent: Kidnapping coercive control or forced marriage
modern_law_note: This would clearly be unlawful under modern UK law
notes: Myth explains the seasonal cycle but the action is deeply troubling by modern standards

modern_uk_equivalent: Kidnapping or no offence depending on consent
modern_law_note: Modern legality would depend heavily on consent coercion and circumstances
notes: Traditions vary over whether Helen was abducted persuaded or willingly left

modern_uk_equivalent: Murder conspiracy to murder or child killing
modern_law_note: Would be treated as an extremely serious criminal offence
notes: Some traditions say Artemis rescues Iphigenia

alleged_action: Refusing to fight during the Trojan War
mythological_punishment: Greek losses and death of Patroclus
modern_law_note: Would depend on military status orders and jurisdiction
notes: Interesting example where mythic blame is not the same as modern criminal liability

modern_uk_equivalent: Unlawful killing or lawful combat depending context
modern_law_note: In modern law this would depend on whether it occurred in lawful armed conflict
notes: Heroic killing in myth but legally complex in modern terms

modern_law_note: Could be serious assault unless justified as self-defence or escape from captivity
notes: Polyphemus had trapped and killed members of Odysseus crew

modern_uk_equivalent: Assault false imprisonment or administering a harmful substance
modern_law_note: Magic is not a legal category but the underlying restraint and harm would be criminal
notes: Useful example for translating mythic magic into modern legal concepts

modern_law_note: Keeping someone against their will would be unlawful
notes: The myth frames this through divine command and desire

modern_law_note: Revenge would not be a lawful defence to murder
notes: Clytemnestra motive is often linked to Iphigenia and Agamemnon actions

modern_law_note: Killing a parent for revenge would be murder
notes: The myth explores competing duties to father mother and divine command

modern_law_note: The killing would be prosecuted rather than treated as ritual pollution
notes: Useful comparison between mythic pollution and formal legal process

modern_law_note: Taking valuable property without permission would generally be unlawful
notes: Heroic quest narratives often treat theft as glory when done by the hero

alleged_action: Helping Jason steal the Golden Fleece
modern_uk_equivalent: Conspiracy theft or aiding an offence
modern_law_note: Helping another person commit theft may create criminal liability
notes: Medea role shifts between helper victim strategist and offender

modern_law_note: Would be treated as murder
notes: One of the darkest mythological episodes

modern_law_note: Would depend whether the Minotaur is treated as a person animal or immediate threat
notes: Excellent messy classification case

alleged_action: Keeping the Minotaur in the Labyrinth
modern_law_note: Detaining a sentient being would be unlawful if treated as a person
notes: The Minotaur classification deliberately creates ambiguity

modern_uk_equivalent: Escape from lawful custody or no offence
modern_law_note: Depends whether the imprisonment was lawful and whether damage occurred
notes: More tragic engineering incident than straightforward crime

alleged_action: Flying too close to the sun
modern_law_note: Recklessness toward oneself is not usually criminal unless it endangers others
notes: Mythic punishment is consequence rather than legal punishment

modern_law_note: Would depend whether Medusa is legally a person and whether there was immediate threat
notes: Another useful monster classification problem

modern_uk_equivalent: Murder manslaughter or self-defence depending facts
modern_law_note: Modern liability would depend on intent knowledge and circumstances
notes: Oedipus does not know Laius is his father

modern_uk_equivalent: Potentially void marriage but criminal liability unclear
modern_law_note: Modern treatment would depend on knowledge consent and specific law
notes: The myth is about pollution fate and identity rather than ordinary criminal intent

modern_uk_equivalent: Potential breach of order but burial itself not usually criminal
modern_law_note: Modern law would not treat family burial duty in the same mythic way
notes: Good example of moral duty conflicting with authority

modern_uk_equivalent: Murder unlawful imprisonment or misconduct in public office
modern_law_note: A ruler ordering death without lawful process would be criminal
notes: Creon is a useful example of state power as harm

alleged_action: Driving Pentheus and Thebes into destruction
modern_law_note: Divine madness has no direct modern legal equivalent but coercion and harm would matter
notes: Useful example of conduct that resists neat legal coding

alleged_action: Entering the Underworld to retrieve Eurydice
modern_law_note: There is no modern equivalent to entering the Underworld
notes: Mostly a tragic rescue attempt rather than a crime
